Pool-exhaustion fallback performs a full `copy.deepcopy(og_tokenizer)` inside `_borrow_from_pool()` on the hot path. The pool is sized to `renderer_num_workers + 1` copies. If concurrent tokenizer calls exceed that (e.g. AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer batching + MM processor + chat-template rendering all sharing the same wrapped tokenizer, or bursty request concurrency > renderer_num_workers), `queue.get_nowait()` raises `queue.Empty` and a fresh Rust-backed tokenizer is deep-copied per call. A tokenizer deepcopy is expensive (clones the Rust backend/vocab), so this converts a rare init-time cost into a repeated per-request cost under load — a latency cliff exactly when concurrency is high. Additionally, the fallback `put`s the extra copy back into the queue in `finally`, so the pool can grow unboundedly beyond `copies`, retaining many large tokenizer copies in memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Concurrency sweep: fire N>renderer_num_workers+1 concurrent encode requests and measure p99 latency + RSS; compare against a bounded/blocking queue.get() variant to quantify deepcopy-on-empty cost and memory growth.

findings.claim: The new `maybe_make_thread_pool` replaces the tokenizer with a `TokenizerPool` subclass whose public methods (`encode`, `decode`, `batch_decode`, `convert_tokens_to_ids`, `__call__`, `apply_chat_template`, etc.) each wrap every invocation in the `_borrow_from_pool()` context manager. This adds per-call overhead — a contextmanager frame plus a `queue.Queue.get_nowait()`/`put()` pair (each taking a lock) — on the tokenization hot path that previously called the tokenizer directly. Because HfRenderer applies this wrapper to `self.tokenizer` (used by AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer for every encode/decode request), this constant per-call cost is repeated on every request's tokenize/detokenize path. Unlike the prior single deepcopy at init, this cost recurs on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark tokenizer.encode()/decode() throughput before vs after wrapping in TokenizerPool, at renderer_num_workers=1 (single-thread) to isolate the queue+contextmanager overhead from concurrency benefit.

In dynamic_quant_epilogue (num_tokens>thread_num branch) the inner loop stride was changed from '++j' to 'j += vec_elem_num'. The parent's '++j' advanced by 1 element while the body loads/stores a full 16-wide vector at offset j, i.e. it iterated ~16x too many times with overlapping vector reads/writes. The diff FIXES a pre-existing perf+correctness bug (over-iteration and redundant overlapping stores); it does NOT introduce a regression. Note this branch was previously only compiled for AVX512; the commit newly enables the AVX2 build, so the fix matters more now.

In NixlEplbCommunicator.execute(), the parent used a persistent `_xfer_cache` keyed by `(src, total_bytes, recv_offset)` so NIXL descriptor lists and prepped xfer handles (get_xfer_descs -> prep_xfer_dlist -> make_prepped_xfer) were built once and reused across every rebalance iteration. The diff removes `_xfer_cache`/`_release_all_cached_handles` and instead calls `_create_peer_xfer()` on every `execute()` call, then releases all local/remote dlist handles and xfer handles in the `finally` block. Since EPLB rebalance runs periodically at runtime and `execute()` is invoked per MoE layer per rebalance step, this reintroduces repeated NIXL descriptor registration/prep + teardown work that was previously amortized by caching — the transfer-setup cost is now paid on every hot-path invocation. This is a memory-vs-setup-cost tradeoff (the PR intentionally reduces buffer memory), but the removal of xfer-handle reuse is a per-iteration setup overhead regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark NixlEplbCommunicator.execute() over repeated rebalance iterations (e.g. 100 iters, fixed expert set) measuring per-call latency, comparing cached-handle reuse vs per-call create+release of NIXL dlist/xfer handles.

findings.claim: This diff is a performance FIX, not a regression. The parent code built a temporary Python list comprehension plus `np.array(..., dtype=np.int32)` on every prepare_next_token_ids_padded call (per spec-decode step), allocating a throwaway numpy array each step. Additionally it materialized `seq_lens_list = (...).tolist()`, another per-step allocation. The new code writes directly into the preallocated `self.backup_next_token_ids.np[i]` buffer element-by-element, eliminating the per-step numpy allocation and the intermediate list. Both versions are host-side scalar loops (get_token_id per request), so the fundamental host-loop cost is unchanged, but the extra transient allocations were removed. No regression introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Spec-decode (EAGLE/MTP) decode-step latency microbenchmark measuring prepare_next_token_ids_padded host overhead across batch sizes (e.g., 1, 32, 256).

findings.claim: This diff is itself the fix for a decode slowdown, not a regression. In the parent snapshot, when building the deepstack IntermediateTensors on the first PP rank, the full persistent buffer `self._ds_buffers[lvl]` (allocated at max_num_tokens) was passed unsliced as `ds_{llm_layer}`. In compiled/CUDA-graph decode where inputs_embeds has n<<max_num_tokens tokens, the consumer loop in Granite4VisionLLMModel.forward hit `buf_len != num_tokens` and executed `torch.nn.functional.pad(feat[:num_tokens], ...)` every layer/step — extra per-step work on the hot decode path (redundant-hot-path-work symptom). The change slices `self._ds_buffers[lvl][:n]` so the tensor matches the token count, avoiding the pad/slice work and the shape mismatch that slowed compiled-mode decode. No new regression is introduced; the diff removes hot-path work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-phase latency for Granite4Vision in compiled/CUDA-graph mode (small batch, single image), comparing per-step time before/after slicing _ds_buffers[lvl][:n].

In execute_in_parallel (multi_stream_utils.py), aux-stream results (kv_score, indexer_kv_score, indexer_weights) are allocated and written on aux_streams[i] and only synchronized to the default stream via done_events[i].wait(). No tensor.record_stream(aux_stream) is called on these aux-produced tensors. The default stream's caching allocator does not know these tensors were used on aux streams, so once the default stream stops referencing them the blocks can be reused while the aux-stream kernel is still in flight — the same cross-stream allocator race as the historical shared_experts clone case (#26440). This is especially acute because attn_gemm_parallel_execute returns aux tensors that are then consumed AGAIN on an aux stream inside the post-GEMM maybe_execute_in_parallel (indexer closure reads indexer_kv_score/indexer_weights on aux_stream_list[0]), so their lifetime spans two separate stream contexts with only event waits, not record_stream, protecting the buffers.

findings.claim: The change removes the `shape_invariants` callbacks (llama_model_invariants / qwen_2_model_invariants) that emitted `torch._check(...)` runtime assertions expressing shape equalities between unbacked symints (e.g. positions.size()[0] == input_ids.size()[0], and hidden/embed dim relations for Qwen2). These equalities are re-expressed only via the new `shape_id="b"` mechanism, which is gated behind `_SUPPORTS_SHAPE_ID = is_torch_equal_or_newer("2.11.0")`. On PyTorch 2.10.x (>=2.10, <2.11) with DynamicShapesType.UNBACKED, shape_id is unsupported, so the shape-relation information is lost entirely: each marked dim gets an independent unbacked symbol with no equality constraint. This mirrors the historical kernel-config-tuning mechanism where providing shape relations/size hints for unbacked symints lets Inductor generate simpler/faster (e.g. 32-bit-indexed, fusible) kernels; dropping the relations forces more conservative codegen and can reintroduce data-dependent guard overhead the invariants were added to avoid.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compile + run LlamaModel/Qwen2Model under dynamic_shapes_config.type=UNBACKED on torch 2.10.x and 2.11+, comparing generated kernel indexing width and per-token decode latency vs the parent (shape_invariants) implementation.

When SP/async-TP is enabled but `use_inductor_graph_partition=False` and piecewise splitting_ops are configured, the new block in vllm/config/compilation.py (~line 1151) forces `self.splitting_ops = []` (switching to full-graph compilation) and, if the requested cudagraph_mode had piecewise cudagraphs, overrides it to `CUDAGraphMode.FULL`. This means a user who requested PIECEWISE or FULL_AND_PIECEWISE cudagraphs will silently lose piecewise cudagraph capture. Piecewise cudagraphs allow attention ops to run outside the graph (dynamic KV/attention) while capturing the rest; forcing full-graph capture can regress workloads whose shapes are not well-captured by full cudagraphs (e.g. prefill/mixed batches with cascade attention that then fall back to eager). This is a config-driven change of the cudagraph capture regime that can miss the piecewise capture opportunity for those batches.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare tokens/sec and per-step latency with enable_sp=True, use_inductor_graph_partition=False, cudagraph_mode=FULL_AND_PIECEWISE on a TP>1 model, before vs after this commit, especially for mixed prefill+decode batches and cascade-attention paths.

findings.claim: The bugfix adds `torch.accelerator.empty_cache()` inside `_ensure_workspace_size`, which is invoked from `get_simultaneous` on the attention/MoE forward path (e.g. flash_attn.py, flashmla_sparse.py, modular_kernel.py, sparse_attn_indexer.py). `empty_cache()` forces the CUDA caching allocator to release all free cached segments back to the driver (a synchronizing, expensive call). While the workspace normally stops growing after warmup and gets locked (so this branch is not hit in steady state), whenever the workspace does grow (elastic EP scaling / first-time reservation per shape) this now triggers a global cache flush. If growth occurs more than a handful of times, this can cause repeated allocator churn and re-cudaMalloc of freed segments, degrading throughput on the forward path. The comment justifies it as reducing peak reserved memory (fixing a deferred-free/reserved-segment retention), which is the intended correctness/memory fix, but the mechanism is a heavyweight synchronous allocator operation on a path shared with hot kernels.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench: repeatedly call get_simultaneous with monotonically increasing sizes (simulate elastic EP grow) and measure wall-clock; compare with/without empty_cache. Also measure end-to-end decode throughput under elastic EP scaling to confirm empty_cache is not hit in the hot path.

The diff removes the local definitions of __HIP__GFX11__ and __HIP__GFX12__ from csrc/rocm/attention.cu (the `#if defined(__gfx1100__)...#define __HIP__GFX11__` and `#define __HIP__GFX12__` blocks) and rewrites the kernel gates to `#elif defined(__GFX11__)` / `#elif defined(__GFX12__)`. But at the parent commit there is NO definition of __GFX11__/__GFX12__ anywhere (grep for those bare symbols returns zero hits; only __HIP__GFX11__/__HIP__GFX12__ existed). Unless the accompanying CMake change (this PR only touches HIP_SUPPORTED_ARCHS in CMakeLists.txt, which does NOT add these defines) or an unshown header defines the bare __GFX11__/__GFX12__ macros for the device compile pass, every RDNA (gfx11/gfx12) build will have these gates evaluate false. That silently drops the vectorized gfx11/gfx12 paged_attention_ll4mi_QKV kernels down to the generic/UNREACHABLE branch — the fast tuned kernel exists but the gate now rejects it. This is exactly the suboptimal-kernel-gate mechanism (a gate that unnecessarily rejects an available fast kernel), and would be a large latency/throughput regression on all gfx11/gfx12 (including the gfx1030/1100/1101/1200/1201 devices), not just the newly added gfx1102/1103.
findings.suggested_benchmark: ROCm paged-attention microbench on gfx1100/gfx1200 (decode latency) comparing this commit vs parent; verify the ll4mi_QKV vectorized kernel path is still selected

findings.claim: In csrc/rocm/skinny_gemms.cu the diff defines the combined RDNA macro via `#if defined(__GFX11__) || defined(__GFX12__)` (for __HIP__GFX1X__) and rewrites all wvSplitKQ fp8 gemm gates from __HIP__GFX12__ to bare __GFX12__. At parent, __HIP__GFX1X__ was defined directly from the __gfxNNNN__ arch predefines and __HIP__GFX12__ was defined locally; the new bare __GFX11__/__GFX12__ symbols are undefined at parent. If those bare macros are not supplied by the build system for the device pass, __HIP__GFX1X__ becomes undefined on all gfx11/gfx12 targets, disabling the optimized wvSplitK/wvSplitKQ skinny-GEMM kernels (DOT2C v_dot2_f32_f16 path and the fp8 dot4 path) and falling to UNREACHABLE_CODE / non-RDNA path. This is a hot GEMM path for low-batch decode; the gate refactor risks routing away from the fast kernel it is meant to enable.

findings.claim: New PyAV `decode_frames` uses a per-frame `container.seek(pts)` + `next(container.decode())` loop. Each seek jumps to the nearest keyframe *before* the target pts and re-primes the decoder. In dense-sampling regimes (e.g. uniform `num_frames=32` or `fps=2` yielding 100+ closely-spaced indices on a large-GOP video), successive seeks land inside the same/adjacent GOPs and force the decoder to redundantly re-decode from each keyframe, so total decoded frames can exceed a single sequential forward pass. This partially reverses the intended 'scale with sampled frames, not video length' win and can be slower than the existing OpenCV sequential grab()/retrieve() path when GOP size >> sampling stride. Since load_bytes runs per-request in the preprocessing thread pool (media/video.py), this is a hot path under serving load. Consider batching/monotonic-forward decode (decode contiguous ranges once, avoiding a seek when the next target lies within the current GOP after the decoder position).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode 32 uniformly-sampled frames from a long (e.g. 5-min, large-GOP) H.264 video via backend='pyav' vs backend='opencv'; compare wall time and total frames decoded.

findings.claim: This commit reverts PR #40331 (the immediate parent), removing the forkserver prewarm path. Specifically it deletes the CLI-side `_bg_prewarm_forkserver` (which called `multiprocessing.set_forkserver_preload(['vllm.v1.engine.async_llm'])` + `forkserver.ensure_running()` early, overlapping the ~3-5s heavy engine/torch import with APIServer argparse/config), and drops `forkserver` from the allowed `VLLM_WORKER_MULTIPROC_METHOD` choices in envs.py. Relative to parent, cold startup that used the opt-in forkserver path now re-pays the full per-worker spawn/import cost (torch + vllm.v1.engine.async_llm) with no prewarm overlap. This is exactly the process-launch-overhead mechanism from the retrieved forkserver-preload prior, but in the removing direction (revert reintroduces the cost).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure `vllm serve <large-model>` cold end-to-end startup time (time-to-ready) with VLLM_WORKER_MULTIPROC_METHOD unset vs forkserver, before and after this revert.

findings.claim: The revert removes `_startup_prefetch_weights` and its invocation in `build_async_engine_client_from_engine_args`. In the parent, a daemon background thread read model weight shards (+ tokenizer/config sidecars) into the OS page cache from the PARENT APIServer, overlapping with the ~10-15s of fork + CUDA context + distributed + model init before EngineCore page-faults on the same files. Removing this reintroduces synchronous cold-disk weight/sidecar read latency in the child during the load phase for large-weight (tens of GB) deployments — a startup-time regression relative to parent. (Mechanism is page-cache prewarm / startup overlap, adjacent to redundant-load; it warms cache before the child does real read work.)
findings.suggested_benchmark: Cold-cache (drop_caches) `vllm serve` startup timing on a large local safetensors model, measuring weight-load phase duration before/after this revert.

The new `_bicubic_resize_and_normalize` helper is decorated with `@torch.compile(dynamic=True)` and is the core work function invoked from CPU multimodal preprocessing (`dynamic_preprocess`, `apply_params`, `video_to_pixel_values`). torch.compile triggers Dynamo tracing + guard/graph compilation lazily on first call, and `dynamic=True` still recompiles when new symbolic-shape/branch combinations (size=None vs size!=None, norm_mean present vs absent, differing dtype) appear. Since images/videos have highly variable resolutions and the two call sites use size=None vs concrete size, this can incur repeated multi-second compile stalls on the preprocessing path, which typically runs on CPU worker threads for a modest amount of numeric work (permute + bicubic interpolate + normalize). Compiling such a light, dynamically-shaped CPU op is a classic case of init-shaped (compile/warmup) work placed on a per-request path; the compile overhead can dominate and regress preprocessing latency versus the previous plain-eager `_bicubic_from_ndarray`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure image+video preprocessing latency (first request cold + steady-state warm) across a mix of resolutions with and without the @torch.compile decorator; watch for recompilation counts via TORCH_LOGS=recompiles.

findings.claim: This diff is a FIX for the blocking-d2h/h2d-sync pattern (matched_leaf: blocking-d2h-sync), not a regression. In embed_input_ids, the parent code called `is_multimodal = is_multimodal.to(input_ids.device)` as a standalone statement. Because the result was immediately used by torch.where in the same synchronous manner, and the .to() was not marked non_blocking, the CPU-side H2D copy of the boolean mask (is_multimodal originates on CPU/host in the mm path) acted as a blocking transfer, stalling the launch stream. The patch fuses the transfer into the torch.where call and adds non_blocking=True, allowing the copy to be issued asynchronously and overlapped. No new GPU/CPU sync is introduced; the change strictly removes a stall. The historical priors (blocking-d2h-sync: pinned/non_blocking async copy for mm placeholder tensors) exactly match, but here the direction is corrective.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark embed_input_ids on a Gemma4 multimodal batch (image tokens present) and profile with nsys/torch profiler to confirm the H2D copy of is_multimodal is now overlapped with the stream and no cudaStreamSynchronize/blocking transfer appears on the launch thread.

In EplbState.step()'s async branch, the guard changed from `if eplb_model_state.pending_global_ready_check: all_ranks_buffer_ready = self._all_ranks_buffer_ready(...)` (a purely local flag gate, only doing the cross-rank all_reduce when a check was actually pending) to `if eplb_model_state.rebalanced and self._all_ranks_result_ready(eplb_model_state)`. Because Python evaluates the `and` left-to-right and `_all_ranks_result_ready` issues a collective `all_reduce` on the ep group, the all_reduce now fires on EVERY forward-pass step while `rebalanced` is True (i.e. for the entire duration of an in-flight rebalance, across all layers), rather than only when a buffer-ready poll was pending. This puts a per-step cross-rank collective (host-side int32 all_reduce, cpu_group or device_group) on the inference critical path for the whole rebalance window. This matches the historically observed 'work repeated every hot-path iteration' pattern.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable async EPLB with ep_size>1 and measure per-forward-pass latency during an active rebalance window (rebalanced=True) vs steady state; count all_reduce calls per step.

findings.claim: The new guard `_is_all_reqs_chunked_prefill()` skips the paired `torch.distributed.broadcast` on both the send side (`_pp_broadcast_..._sampled_token_ids`) and the receive side (`_pp_receive_prev_sampled_token_ids_to_input_batch`). Correctness/hang-safety here depends on ALL PP ranks evaluating `_is_all_reqs_chunked_prefill()` identically for the same micro-batch. This predicate reads `self.discard_request_mask.np[:num_reqs]`, which is populated locally per-rank from the scheduler output. If any rank's `discard_request_mask`/`num_reqs` view diverges (e.g. num_reqs==0 edge case where `.all()` on empty array returns True, or a rank where the mask is not yet synced to the current micro-batch), the sender and receiver disagree on whether to broadcast — one side posts a collective the peer never matches, producing a deadlock/stall rather than a fast path. This is the classic boolean-guard-misfire mechanism: a runtime predicate gating a collective that must be evaluated consistently across all participants.
findings.suggested_benchmark: PP>1 with async scheduling and chunked prefill: mix batches where some ranks see all-chunked and others do not (e.g. num_reqs==0 on some rank); verify no NCCL timeout/hang and measure step latency.

findings.claim: This diff FIXES a piecewise-cudagraph dispatch-key mismatch in the P/W + LoRA case, rather than introducing a regression. During PIECEWISE capture, create_forward_fn/forward_fn previously built BatchDescriptor(num_tokens=num_tokens) with the default has_lora=False, so when LoRA-specialized graphs (has_lora=True) are captured the runtime dispatch key would not match at replay time. Because BatchDescriptor is frozen and dispatching keys on has_lora (added as a specialization dimension per the 'suboptimal-kernel-gate'/LoRA-specialized-cudagraph history), the capture key without has_lora would fail to match the runtime lookup, causing the model to silently fall through to the eager (CUDAGraphMode.NONE) path for LoRA batches — a gpu-util/latency regression from missed cudagraph replay. Passing has_lora=has_lora into the capture-time BatchDescriptor aligns capture and dispatch keys so the piecewise graph is actually replayed. No new regression is introduced by this change; it removes one.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable LoRA with PIECEWISE cudagraph mode (P/W) and verify captured piecewise graphs are dispatched/replayed for LoRA batches (assert cudagraph_runtime_mode != NONE at replay); compare decode latency vs pre-fix eager fallback.

static rule 'early-exit-misses-group-none-guard' matched antipattern: An early-exit "fast path" checks only a size==1 condition but omits the group/handle-is-None case, so when the collective is disabled the code falls through and does expensive allocation+copy work that is then discarded because the actual collective is separately guarded on the None check. (matched: 'if ep_world_size == 1:')

The new torchembed fused RoPE branch in apply_rotary_pos_emb inserts two explicit layout-reordering copies on a hot path (per-q and per-k, per layer, per step). `t_kernel = t.movedim(0, -2).contiguous()` materializes a fresh contiguous copy of the entire (sliced) tensor to move the seq dim to position -2, and `t = t_out.movedim(-2, 0).contiguous()` materializes another full copy to restore the original layout. The original reference path was two fused elementwise multiply-adds with NO layout/copy kernels. So even in the intended-happy fused case (torchembed installed + CUDA + even rot_dim), the function now pays for two extra full-tensor transpose/copy kernels around the triton kernel, which can easily erase (or exceed) the fused-kernel speedup versus the plain elementwise path — a classic wasteful transpose/copy overhead regression. This is the same mechanism as the MLA _concat_k_nope_k_pe historical case: forcing materialization via non-native layout ops around a kernel.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark apply_rotary_pos_emb on CUDA with torchembed installed vs the reference elementwise path for typical SP shapes [seq, batch, heads, head_dim] (e.g. seq=4096, heads=32, head_dim=128); measure wall time and kernel count (nsys/torch profiler) to confirm the two movedim(...).contiguous() copies do not dominate.

findings.claim: This diff is a FIX for the deferred-free-peak pattern, not an introduction of a regression. In _linear_backward (linear.py ~line 1231) and _LayerNormLinear backward (layernorm_linear.py ~line 1052), the parent code only called clear_tensor_data(grad_output) for the row-parallel SP case. The column-parallel SP FP8 path quantizes grad_output into a Float8TensorStorage that was internally allocated but never freed before return, relying on GC and raising peak memory (and leaving live cudagraph-pool tensors under torch.compile reduce-overhead). The diff extends the guard to also cover `parallel_mode == 'column' and fp8`, promptly freeing the quantized grad_output. This directly matches the retrieved deferred-free-peak leaf and correctly remedies it. No regression introduced; grad_output is not consumed after the wgrad GEMM in the column-SP branch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Column-parallel + sequence-parallel FP8 Linear/LayerNormLinear backward peak-memory test; verify quantized grad_output is freed before return (and no lingering cudagraph-pool tensors under torch.compile mode='reduce-overhead').

In DotProductAttention.forward (hot path, invoked every attention call), the new `_pad_qkv_head_dim` calls `F.pad` on query_layer and key_layer (and value_layer) whenever `head_dim_qk != head_dim_v` and the FA2 backend is selected. `F.pad` materializes a brand-new zero-filled tensor and copies the entire Q/K (and possibly V) contents on each forward — a full extra allocation + copy of the QKV tensors per iteration. For THD/MLA workloads where head_dim_qk != head_dim_v is the steady-state config, this padding+copy happens on every step, adding memory traffic proportional to the QKV size before the FlashAttention kernel. This is the classic 'work repeated every iteration' pattern the historical repeat_interleave/repeat cases flagged.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DotProductAttention forward with THD layout, head_dim_qk != head_dim_v (e.g. MLA qk=192,v=128), FA2 backend, measure per-iteration latency/throughput vs parent (which fell back to fused attention).

findings.claim: `_trim_output` performs reshape -> slice `[..., :orig_head_dim_v]` -> reshape on the FlashAttention output on the hot path when orig_qk_dim > orig_v_dim. The narrow-slice followed by reshape forces a contiguity copy of the output tensor each forward, discarding the padded columns produced by the padded-V attention. This is extra copy work introduced per iteration to compensate for the up-front padding.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark MLA config where head_dim_qk > head_dim_v under FA2 THD path and measure output-trim overhead.

The new NVFP4 SReLU backward path adds a re-quantization step that the MXFP8 path does not need. cuDNN's grouped dSReLU FP4 kernel recomputes the FC2 input in BF16, so the diff now materializes fc2_x_bf16 = d_srelu_tensor.view(...).contiguous() and runs a full extra _group_quantize_for_grouped_mlp on it (columnwise NVFP4 quantize over all tokens) before the wgrad GEMM. This is an added per-backward-step grouped-quantize kernel launch + BF16 contiguous copy on the hot MoE backward path, whereas the MXFP8 branch consumes the columnwise scales that the DSReLU kernel already returned (sfd_col_d_srelu_tensor) with no extra quantize. This is effectively the inverse of a fusion: the recompute-in-BF16 + separate re-quantize is two kernels where MXFP8 got the quantized output fused out of the dSReLU kernel.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Backward pass of NVFP4 grouped MLP with ScaledSReLU (weight_requires_grad=True, recompute_input_from_dsrelu path) vs MXFP8 SReLU; measure added grouped-quantize kernel time and BF16 contiguous copy overhead per step across num_groups.

The final commit ('drop ctx.backward_objects after backward') is a memory *improvement*, not a regression: it nulls ctx.backward_objects immediately after _linear_backward so saved tensors/quantizers/weakrefs/main_grad closures are released instead of surviving until ctx GC under retain_graph=True. No shared-workspace-reuse contention pattern is introduced (the FP8 weight_workspace cache handling is preserved: workspace is returned alongside out and refreshed by the module, still not autograd-tracked).

findings.claim: This diff FIXES (does not introduce) a suboptimal-kernel-gate regression that existed in the parent. In the parent, the SM90 FA3-over-FA4 preference block was gated as `if use_flash_attention_4 and use_flash_attention_3 and device_compute_capability == (9,0)` and then unconditionally set `use_flash_attention_4 = False`. Crucially, `use_flash_attention_3` is only the NVTE_FLASH_ATTN flag (defaults to 1 and is only zeroed for cc != (9,0)), NOT `v3_is_installed`. So on SM90 with FA4 installed but FA3 NOT installed, the parent code still disabled FA4 to 'prefer FA3', causing fallback to slower FA2/cuDNN even though FA4 (the faster available Hopper kernel) was present. The fix adds `FlashAttentionUtils.v3_is_installed` (and `v4_is_installed`) to the guard so FA4 is only demoted when FA3 is genuinely installed. Direction check confirms the diff removes a wrong gate rather than adding one — no new regression introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On SM90 with flash-attn-4 installed but FA3 (flashattn-hopper) NOT installed, run get_attention_backend for a standard DPA config and assert flash_attention_backend selects FA4; benchmark DPA fwd/bwd throughput vs FA2/cuDNN fallback to confirm FA4 is chosen.

verify.claim: This diff FIXES (does not introduce) a suboptimal-kernel-gat
verify.reason: 机制与方向都站得住：parent 的守卫确实只检查 use_flash_attention_3 标志而非 v3_is_installed，因此 FA3 未安装、FA4 已安装时仍会把 FA4 关掉回退

findings.claim: This diff is the *fix* for the historical THD O(T^2) mask-materialization case, not a regression: the old slow path built full Q×KV masks via make_attention_mask (segment_mask, segment_mask_with_id, bottom_right/causal masks) then reduced with _mask_to_seqlens_offset — O(T·T) work/memory. The new _get_seqlens_offsets_thd replaces it with per-segment one-hot scatter + min/max aggregation that is O(T·max_segments_per_seq). For THD workloads where max_segments_per_seq << T this is a strict asymptotic reduction. This matches historical leaf algorithmic-compute-reduction but in the improving direction, so no regression is reported for this path.

The new variable-shape grouped swizzle kernel (grouped_swizzle_scaling_variable_shape_kernel) replaces the direct 3D grid launch (blockIdx.z = tensor index, one block does one tile) with a persistent-grid scheme that assigns a linear_block_id and then, for EVERY iteration of the persistent loop, re-discovers the owning tensor via an O(num_tensors) linear scan over m_array/k_array, recomputing round_up_to_multiple / DIVUP / vec_load_size / grid dims per tensor from scratch. Every one of these iterations also reads m_array[i] and k_array[i] from device global memory. This makes per-block work O(persistent_iterations * num_tensors) instead of O(1) tile lookup. For large group counts (many experts) this quadratic-ish scan can dominate the actual swizzle transpose work, which is memory-bound and cheap. This is a parallel-decomposition rewrite (persistent grid + on-device scheduler), matching the kernel-occupancy-redesign mechanism where the decomposition itself changed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Grouped swizzle with large num_tensors (e.g. 128/256 experts) and small per-tensor M/K, comparing uniform-shape fast path vs variable-shape path throughput

findings.claim: The Naive/Radix top-K dispatch gate uses a single scalar threshold `if (topk < 16)` on the *overall* topk, but in the group_topk (group-limited) branch `topk_and_mask<TopkFunc>` is invoked with much smaller effective K values (`topk/group_topk` for per-group selection, and `group_topk` for group selection). When `topk >= 16` triggers TopkFuncType::Radix for the whole kernel, these small-K sub-selections are forced onto the Radix path even though their effective K is well below the crossover (e.g. topk=32, group_topk=4 -> per-group K=8, group K=4). Radix always executes a fixed 8 passes over the data plus 2 gather passes (~10 O(E) sweeps with per-bucket warp_allreduce_sum over 16 buckets each pass), so for these tiny-K calls the naive O(K^2*E) path would actually be cheaper. The gate rejects the faster kernel for a subset of the calls based on a coarse global threshold rather than per-call effective K. This is the classic 'fast kernel exists but gating logic picks the wrong one' mechanism.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark fused_topk_with_score_function forward with group_topk enabled (e.g. num_experts=256, num_groups=8, topk=32, group_topk=4) comparing Naive vs Radix for the per-group sub-selections; measure kernel latency vs topk<16 baseline.

In rocm_aiter_ops.hc_head (new in vllm/_aiter_ops.py), each call allocates full_fn (shape hc_mult3 x hc_mult*hidden_size), full_base (hc_mult3), and full_scale (3) via torch.zeros(...) on device, then writes the real fn/base/scale into the leading rows/slots. These are padded copies of parameters that are constant across forward steps. hc_head is invoked in the compute_logits/MTP hot path (deepseek_v4.py hc_head() and deepseek_v4_mtp.py), so the zero-init of a full (hc_mult3, hc_mult*hidden_size) fp32 buffer plus copies is redundant per-token/per-step work on a correctly-sizable-but-recomputed buffer. Recommend precomputing/caching full_fn/full_base/full_scale once at weight-load time rather than re-zeroing+copying every call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark hc_head on ROCm across decode batch sizes (1,8,64), comparing per-call zero+copy of full_fn vs a cached padded buffer; measure logits-computation latency.

findings.claim: This diff changes output_is_reduced() from False->True in both flashinfer_nvlink one/two-sided PrepareAndFinalize backends. Per moe_runner.py, _fused_output_is_reduced is derived directly from output_is_reduced(). This is a bugfix eliminating a *double reduce*: previously output_is_reduced()==False caused _maybe_reduce_final_output() to issue an additional tensor_model_parallel_all_reduce over the combined MoE output, even though the FlashInfer NVLink combine (mnnvl_moe_alltoallv_combine / moe_alltoall.combine) already produces a reduced result. The change is therefore performance-POSITIVE (removes a redundant all-reduce collective on the hot MoE finalize path); it does NOT introduce a regression.

When fsdp_db_use_persist_buf_on_alloc_fail defaults to False (the shipped default), any bucket whose FSDP unit does not fit the FixedPoolAllocator falls through to self.backup_allocator (TemporaryBucketAllocator), i.e. dynamic per-step CUDA memory allocation for the all-gather/reduce-scatter temporary buffers. This dynamic-allocation path is not eligible for NCCL user-buffer (nccl_ub) registration, so those collectives run on the slower non-UB path. The commit's own docstring states this 'usually leads to low performance.' The new opt-in flag mitigates it, but because the default is False, models with a bucket that overflows the fixed double-buffer pool silently keep the low-performance dynamic-allocator path on the hot forward/backward comm path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: FSDP training with a model containing at least one bucket that exceeds the FixedPoolAllocator double-buffer size, with nccl_ub=True and fsdp_db_use_persist_buf_on_alloc_fail=False vs True; measure all-gather/reduce-scatter throughput and step time.

clip_qk() issues a separate torch.distributed.all_reduce (ReduceOp.MAX) per transformer layer over the data-parallel(+CP) group inside the optimizer step. With N decoder layers this is N small collectives every training iteration on the critical path between optimizer.step() and the next forward. These per-layer tiny all-reduces (payload = num_heads floats) are latency-bound and not batched; they could be coalesced into a single all_reduce over a stacked tensor across all layers to reduce collective launch overhead. This resembles the historical per-call collective-payload pattern where the number/frequency of collectives on the hot path was the cost driver.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Per-iteration step time with --qk-clip enabled on a model with many layers across multiple DP ranks; measure all_reduce launch overhead (N layers x per-iter).

findings.claim: The new FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel is inserted at the FRONT of _POSSIBLE_NVFP4_KERNELS[PlatformEnum.CUDA], ahead of FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel and CutlassNvFp4LinearKernel. Since is_supported() only requires SM10x + FlashInfer and can_implement() unconditionally returns True, on every SM100 device with FlashInfer the auto-selected NVFP4 linear backend silently changes from cutlass to cute-dsl. This is exactly the fused-backend-swap risk pattern: the default GEMM implementation for all NVFP4 linear layers is replaced by a prebuilt library kernel whose per-shape performance profile may differ from cutlass (which had been chosen as the top auto candidate). If the cute-dsl GEMM is slower for common decode/prefill shapes, this becomes a broad throughput/latency regression across all NVFP4 models on SM100 without any opt-in.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark NVFP4 linear/GEMM latency and end-to-end decode+prefill throughput on SM100 (B200) for a range of M (batch*seq) and typical N/K, comparing default auto-selection before vs after this commit (cutlass vs cute-dsl).

findings.claim: FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel does NOT override input_quant_key() (inherits the base default returning None) and its apply_weights() always performs its own scaled_fp4_quant on the activation. In contrast, FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel overrides input_quant_key() to return kNvfp4Dynamic and consumes a pre-quantized QuantizedActivation via as_quantized_activation, letting an upstream fused op supply the already-quantized activation and skip the per-layer input quantization. Because cute-dsl is now the front-of-list auto default, models that would previously benefit from the fused input-quant path (QuantizedActivation) lose that fusion: the input quantization is no longer folded/skipped and is re-executed inside every layer. This is the kernel-fusion regression mechanism (an existing input-quant + GEMM fusion opportunity is dropped) triggered by making the non-fusing backend the default.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On SM100, compare per-token latency of NVFP4 linear layers with and without the QuantizedActivation input-quant fusion path (cutlass with input_quant_key vs cute-dsl re-quantizing), measuring the cost of the redundant scaled_fp4_quant call in the decode hot path.

use_persistent is computed as `not is_chunked_continuation.any()` over the whole batch. In hybrid batches that mix decode requests with even a single chunked-prefill continuation, the entire batch — including the pure-decode requests that would be numerically correct and benefit from the persistent kernel — is forced onto the slower non-persistent path. Since decode requests dominate steady-state serving throughput, a single mixed continuation can broaden the slow-path fallback beyond what correctness strictly requires. Consider a per-request/per-token routing instead of a batch-wide .any() so decode entries keep the persistent kernel.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Mixed decode+chunked-prefill batch throughput: measure decode-token latency when 1 chunked-prefill continuation is co-scheduled, before/after this change.

In `TokenizeParams.get_encode_kwargs`, the condition that disables tokenizer(HF)-level truncation was broadened from `self.truncation_side == "left"` to `self.truncation_side is not None and self.truncate_prompt_tokens is not None`. This means requests explicitly setting `truncation_side="right"` now also skip fast tokenizer-level truncation and instead encode the ENTIRE prompt (returning `truncation=False`), only to slice it down to `max_length` afterwards in the pure-Python `_token_truncation`. For very long inputs that are meant to be truncated to a small N, this forces tokenization of the full sequence (O(full_len)) plus an extra Python slice per request, whereas the old right-side path let the tokenizer stop at max_length. The extra cost is per-request (a tokenization call sits on the request path). Impact is bounded to requests that opt into `truncation_side` explicitly (default is None -> unchanged), so severity is low, but it is a real added per-request cost on the tokenize path for right-side truncation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark completion/chat request latency with a very long prompt and `truncate_prompt_tokens=N, truncation_side="right"` vs no truncation_side, comparing tokenizer encode time (full-sequence encode vs truncated encode).

findings.claim: In launch_cooperative_cluster (cooperative_topk.cu), cudaFuncSetAttribute is called on EVERY launch: cudaFuncAttributeMaxDynamicSharedMemorySize (always) and cudaFuncAttributeNonPortableClusterSizeAllowed (for CS>8). These are host-side driver calls that need only be set once per (kernel, attribute) pair. Because launch_cooperative_topk_impl is invoked from the DSA indexer DECODE path (per decode step, a hot low-latency path — the very scenario the kernel targets), this adds a redundant driver-side round trip on every launch, eating into the latency budget the kernel is meant to reduce. Compare with topk.cu / persistent_topk.cuh which gate cudaFuncSetAttribute behind a macro/once guard rather than per-call. Recommend caching the attribute-set with a static/once flag keyed by (CS,TopK).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench cooperative_topk launch overhead: time N=1000 back-to-back launches with num_rows=1..32 vs a variant that sets cudaFuncSetAttribute once; measure per-launch host latency delta on a decode-shaped input.

findings.claim: The default (auto) unquantized MoE backend selection on CUDA changes semantics: at parent, when neither VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER_MOE_FP16 nor VLLM_FLASHINFER_MOE_BACKEND was set, the oracle deterministically fell back to TRITON (FlashInfer paths were strictly opt-in). After the diff, the priority list is [FLASHINFER_TRTLLM, FLASHINFER_CUTLASS, TRITON, ...] and when VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER_MOE_FP16 is not set the FP16-handling block is skipped entirely, so the code falls through to iterating AVAILABLE_BACKENDS in priority order and will now auto-select FLASHINFER_TRTLLM / FLASHINFER_CUTLASS whenever is_supported_config passes on Blackwell. This flips a previously opt-in fast/experimental path into the default. If the newly-preferred kernel is slower/less-stable for a given shape than TRITON (the historical default), this is a silent throughput/latency change driven by default backend ordering rather than an explicit user toggle.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare tokens/s and TTFT for an unquantized BF16 MoE model (e.g. Mixtral/Qwen3 MoE) on SM100 with moe_backend=auto and no FlashInfer env vars set, parent vs this commit, to detect whether the default kernel silently switched from TRITON to FlashInfer TRTLLM/CUTLASS and whether it regresses.

findings.claim: enable_allreduce_rms_fusion now returns False whenever pipeline_parallel_size > 1, even when TP>1 on Hopper/Blackwell with FlashInfer installed. This disables the fused FlashInfer allreduce+RMSNorm kernel for PP>1 configurations, causing those deployments to fall back to the unfused all-reduce + RMSNorm path (extra kernel launches / a round-trip that the fusion eliminated). This is an intentional deadlock bugfix (the fused op's GPU-side peer-signal spin-wait can diverge across independently-warmed TP subgroups), so the correctness rationale is sound. However, the perf trade-off is real: any TP>1 & PP>1 run on supported hardware loses the allreduce_rms fusion throughput benefit until the underlying warmup-divergence issue is fixed and the gate can be relaxed. Matches the 'config toggle routes away from a fused perf feature' prior mechanism, though here the toggle is the fix rather than a regression per se.
findings.suggested_benchmark: TP=2,PP=2 vs TP=4,PP=1 throughput on H100/B200 with FlashInfer at -O2/-O3, comparing fuse_allreduce_rms on/off to quantify the fusion loss for PP>1

The auto-selection logic in ParallelConfig now defaults the EPLB weight-exchange communicator to 'torch_gloo' whenever eplb_config.use_async is set (and not elastic EP). torch_gloo (TorchDistGlooStagedEplbCommunicator) routes all expert-weight P2P transfers through the CPU with D2H staging copies + full stream synchronize per execute(), and issues gloo (TCP/host) sends instead of NCCL GPU-direct/NVLink. This is a substantially slower default path than the previous behavior (NCCL batch_isend_irecv on device), and it is chosen by a static config default rather than by measured need. Async EPLB users previously used NCCL P2P directly; now they silently get CPU-staged gloo transfers unless they explicitly override communicator='torch_nccl'/'pynccl'. This is the classic 'static default routes to the slow path' pattern.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark async EPLB weight-exchange latency comparing communicator='torch_gloo' (new default) vs 'torch_nccl' on a multi-GPU MoE model; measure per-layer transfer time and end-to-end rebalance latency.

findings.claim: The diff flips the static default `enable_flashinfer_autotune` from True to False in OPTIMIZATION_LEVEL_01 and OPTIMIZATION_LEVEL_02 (O2 is the default optimization_level). This disables FlashInfer's kernel autotuning, forcing FlashInfer to fall back to untuned/heuristic kernel configurations instead of the empirically-selected best kernels — a latency/throughput regression on FlashInfer paths (attention/GEMM). The change is intentional and motivated by an upstream correctness bug (flashinfer-ai/flashinfer#3197), so it is a correctness-forced slow path rather than a stray misconfiguration. It should be re-enabled once the upstream issue is fixed. Note O3 was left unchanged in this diff (still True), leaving an inconsistency across levels.

In vllm/envs.py `use_aot_compile()`, the AOT-compile default gate was changed from `is_torch_equal_or_newer("2.10.0.dev")` to `is_torch_equal_or_newer("2.11.0.dev")`. All the *other* 2.10.0.dev guards in this PR were bumped to "2.10.0" (final) to enable those features on the new Torch 2.10 release, but this one guard was raised to 2.11.0.dev instead of 2.10.0. Net effect: on Torch 2.10.0 (the release this PR ships), the default value for VLLM_USE_AOT_COMPILE flips from "1" (was true for >=2.10.0.dev, i.e. 2.10 builds) to "0", disabling AOT compile by default. This is a static default-value change that routes users onto the slower (non-AOT) compile path unless they explicitly set VLLM_USE_AOT_COMPILE=1 — a compile/startup-time regression relative to prior 2.10 behavior. Confirm whether the intent was to keep AOT default-on for 2.10.0.

In VllmConfig.__post_init__ (vllm/config/vllm.py, the new block after line 597), when `kv_transfer_config is not None` the code now force-sets `scheduler_config.disable_hybrid_kv_cache_manager = True`. This unconditionally turns OFF the hybrid KV cache manager (HMA allocator) for ANY KV connector deployment (NIXL, LMCache, shared-storage, CPU offload, MultiConnector, disaggregated prefill/decode). HMA gives right-sized per-layer-type KV block allocation (full vs sliding-window vs Mamba attention); disabling it forces the uniform (non-hybrid) allocator, which over-allocates KV memory for sliding-window/Mamba models and reduces effective KV capacity/throughput. This is a static config-toggle that selects the slow/less-memory-efficient path regardless of whether the connector actually supports HMA. Note that a HMA-support check already exists in KVConnectorFactory.create_connector (factory.py line ~59: `supports_hma(connector_cls)`), so a more targeted gate (only disable for connectors that don't subclass SupportsHMA) would avoid the blanket regression — as the TODO in the diff itself acknowledges.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare KV cache memory footprint / max concurrent sequences / throughput for a sliding-window model (e.g. gemma-2 or Mistral SWA) and a Mamba/hybrid model with a KV connector enabled (e.g. NixlConnector or LMCacheConnectorV1) before vs after this commit: HMA-on vs forced HMA-off.

The main sampling kernel `_gumbel_sample_kernel` now casts the entire per-block logits vector to FP64 (`logits = logits.to(tl.float64)`) and performs the gumbel-noise add and the `tl.max` argmax reduction over the whole vocabulary in FP64. `gumbel_sample` is on the per-decode-step sampling hot path (called from Sampler.sample() every step, and from the eagle speculator draft loop). On typical NVIDIA datacenter GPUs FP64 arithmetic throughput is a small fraction of FP32 (often 1/2 on A100/H100 real FP64 units, and 1/32–1/64 on consumer GPUs), so converting a vocab-wide (num_tokens × vocab_size) elementwise+reduction pass from FP32 to FP64 roughly doubles/greatly increases the compute cost and register pressure of the sampling kernel — a latency regression proportional to vocab_size on every generated token.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark _gumbel_sample_kernel latency at vocab_size~128k-256k for a range of num_tokens (batch sizes), comparing parent (FP32) vs this commit (FP64), on both A100/H100 and a consumer GPU; also end-to-end decode throughput/ITL for random-sampling (temp>0) workloads.

findings.claim: `tl_rand64` replaces a single `tl.rand()` with `tl.randint4x` plus uint32/uint64 bitcasts, a 64-bit shift/or, and an FP64 multiply, generating a full 64-bit uniform. This is intrinsically more instructions per element than the previous FP32 `tl.rand` path, and in the gumbel kernel it is invoked over the full block (vocab-wide). Combined with the FP64 cast this compounds the per-token sampling cost. In the rejection kernel it is only a per-step scalar so the added RNG cost there is negligible.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Isolate RNG-noise generation cost: tl.rand(FP32) vs tl_rand64 over a 1024-wide block within _gumbel_sample_kernel.

In DFlashQwen3Model.precompute_and_store_context_kv (vllm/model_executor/models/qwen3_dflash.py), the K/V precompute path runs several Python-level per-layer loops on the hot path: (1) `for i in range(L): ops.rms_norm(all_k_normed[i], all_k[i], ...)` for K-norm, and (2) `for i in range(L): attn.impl.do_kv_cache_update(...)` for cache inserts. The docstring explicitly notes this function is NOT covered by torch.compile / CUDA graphs (because context shape differs from query shape), so each of these L iterations issues eager per-layer kernel launches every drafting step. For deep target models (L = target_layer_count) this adds L separate launch batches per step. The commit already fuses the KV GEMM and RoPE across layers; the remaining per-layer RMSNorm and cache-insert loops are the residual launch-overhead cost. This is inherent to the design but worth flagging as a hot-path per-iteration cost.

In execute_in_parallel, hidden_states is read on multiple aux streams (aux_fns[0..2] all consume `hidden_states` via cublas_gemm/weights_proj) concurrently with fused_wqa_wkv on the default stream, but there is no torch.cuda.Event fan-in ordering issue on the *inputs*: start_event.record() happens BEFORE default_fn is launched, and each aux stream waits on start_event, so producers of hidden_states on the default stream that ran before start_event.record() are correctly ordered. However, hidden_states is only kept alive by the caller's Python reference on the default stream; the aux-stream GEMMs read it without record_stream(). If hidden_states were freed/reused on the default stream while an aux GEMM is still in flight (e.g. under CUDA caching-allocator pressure), this reproduces the shared-mutable-state race pattern from #26440 (clone-on-aux-stream without record_stream). Recommend record_stream() on hidden_states for each aux stream used, or documenting that hidden_states outlives all done_events.

This diff is a REMEDIATION of the historical 'one-time-setup-on-hot-path' pattern, not a regression. The old path (DNNLPrimitiveHelper::gemm_s8s8_jit in the deleted dnnl_helper.hpp) reconstructed a dnnl::matmul::primitive_desc and instantiated `dnnl::matmul matmul(matmul_pd)` on EVERY GEMM call, and (on aarch64) also created memory descriptors and reordered/repacked the weight per call. The new W8A8MatMulPrimitiveHandler moves all of this to one-time setup: (1) weight is prepacked ONCE in the constructor via prepack_weight()/dnnl::reorder and cached in memory_cache_[DNNL_ARG_WEIGHTS]; (2) matmul primitives are memoized in a two-level DNNLPrimitiveCache (ClassMatmulCache -> MSizeCache keyed by M size), so execute() reuses cached primitives; (3) runtime memory storages are pre-built once (init_runtime_memory_cache) and only the data handle + dims[0] are patched per call in execute(). This directly eliminates the per-call primitive/reorder cost that was the historical latency symptom.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CPU W8A8 (int8) linear latency: run tests/kernels/test_onednn.py and a decode-loop microbench comparing per-call GEMM latency before/after; verify the primitive cache hit path avoids matmul reconstruction.

The new parent-side _startup_prefetch_weights reads ALL weight shards (*.safetensors + *.bin) into the OS page cache using its own ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=8, 16MB blocks). The engine's existing in-child prefetch (weight_utils._prefetch_all_checkpoints) already does the same thing, but shards the file set across TP ranks (paths_to_prefetch = sorted_files[rank::world_size]). The parent process has no rank awareness and unconditionally reads the FULL file set. On a fresh/cold page cache this is largely complementary (parent warms cache, child page-faults into it), but when the model does NOT fit in RAM (the very case where in-child prefetch is intentionally skipped: `should_prefetch and not fits_in_ram` disables prefetch), the parent still reads every shard, evicting earlier shards and forcing the child to re-read from disk — a wasted duplicate scan of tens of GB. The parent prefetch ignores the fits_in_ram / auto-prefetch gating that the in-child logic uses, so it can do redundant I/O the in-child path deliberately avoids.
findings.suggested_benchmark: serve startup wall-clock for a >RAM-sized model on cold cache: compare time-to-first-weight-loaded with/without parent prefetch; monitor page-cache eviction / disk read bytes.

findings.claim: The new admission gate `can_fit_full_sequence` runs on the per-request WAITING-loop hot path (scheduler.py ~line 725, inside `while (self.waiting or self.skipped_waiting) and token_budget > 0`). It calls `coordinator.get_num_blocks_to_allocate(...)` with the FULL sequence length (`full_num_tokens = min(request.num_tokens, max_model_len)`), which iterates over every single_type_manager / kv_cache_group. Immediately afterward `allocate_slots` calls the same `get_num_blocks_to_allocate` again (this time for the chunk). So each admitted request now performs a duplicate per-group block-accounting pass every scheduling step it is still in prefill. For hybrid models with many kv_cache_groups this doubles that accounting work on the scheduler critical path. Since the flag defaults to True (`scheduler_reserve_full_isl: bool = True`), this cost is incurred by default for all configs, not opt-in.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Scheduler throughput microbench: chunked-prefill with many small waiting requests and a hybrid (full+sliding) kv cache, measure schedule() latency / tokens/s with scheduler_reserve_full_isl True vs False.

findings.claim: Adding an unbounded `@lru_cache` (no maxsize) to `supports_kw` in vllm/utils/__init__.py caches on `(callable, kw_name, ...)`. The first positional arg is a `Callable` — in the observed call sites (`interfaces_base.py._check_vllm_model_init` uses `model.__init__`, `_check_vllm_model_forward` uses `getattr(model, 'forward')`, and `interfaces.py` uses `model_forward`). When called on model *instances* (the `object` overloads allow instances, not only classes), `model.__init__`/`model.forward` are bound methods whose hash/eq incorporate the instance (`__self__`). Every distinct instance therefore produces a new cache entry that is never evicted, and — crucially for memory — the `lru_cache` holds a strong reference to the bound method (and thus the model/module instance) as part of the key, preventing GC. Over many requests/models this is monotonic unbounded growth of a global cache holding live model objects, matching the memory-leak-unpruned-state mechanism (same class as the FA cu_seqlens/lru_cache case). This is precisely the risk that motivated using bounded caches elsewhere in this file (see the custom bounded `LRUCache` class).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instantiate many distinct model objects (or repeatedly wrap/reload models) and call is_vllm_model()/is_text_generation_model() on the instances; measure supports_kw.cache_info() growth and RSS over iterations to confirm entries (and referenced instances) are never freed.

findings.claim: The new eagle spec-decode gate computes `input_fits_in_drafter = spec_decode_common_attn_metadata.seq_lens.max() + num_speculative_tokens <= effective_drafter_max_model_len`. In CommonAttentionMetadata, `seq_lens` is the GPU device tensor (populated via non-blocking H2D copy `self.seq_lens.copy_to_gpu()`), while `seq_lens_cpu` is the host mirror. Calling `.max()` on the GPU `seq_lens` tensor and comparing it in Python forces a blocking GPU->CPU synchronization on every model step of the EAGLE spec-decode path (a hot per-step path). This introduces a device-to-host sync stall that did not exist before the change. The code should read from `seq_lens_cpu.max()` instead to avoid the D2H sync.

register_z3_param now issues a blocking ProcessGroup::allgather + ->wait() PER PARAMETER at registration time (init_z3.py loops `for p in engine.module.parameters(): dc.register_z3_param(...)`). For a model with thousands of parameters this adds one small collective per param during setup. Each collective is a serialized single-int64 allgather that cannot be batched/overlapped and forces a device round-trip. This is a validation-only collective (result is only used to throw on mismatch), so it is pure setup overhead that scales O(num_params). It is well-formed on all ranks but every call is essentially unnecessary work that could be batched into one collective or dropped.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure init_z3 registration wall-time for a large parameter-count model (e.g. many-layer transformer) with world_size>=2, comparing parent vs this commit.

findings.claim: The registration-time verification copies each rank's gathered count to CPU synchronously via `all_padded_counts[i].to(torch::kCPU).item<int64_t>()` inside a per-rank loop, once per parameter. This diff switches DeepCompile's ZeRO3 joint-graph partitioner from a custom save-selection strategy to min_cut_rematerialization_partition, driven by marking parameter-alias / cast nodes as MUST_RECOMPUTE. The PR's own preliminary evaluation confirms this trades some throughput for memory: eager+autocast 551.92->571.24ms (+3.5%), eager+bf16 419.87->445.76ms (+6.2%), inductor+autocast 546.97->570.09ms (+4.2%). The regression is intentional (memory-for-time) and aligns with the historical skip-saved-tensor-backward mechanism (that pattern's symptom was memory; here the previous custom partitioner over-saved gathered/downcasted params). Because MUST_RECOMPUTE forces recomputation (via fused cast + allgather) in the backward, the extra device-time is expected. The docstring TODO(eternalNight) itself flags that min_cut 'may recompute more nodes than required for ZeRO3', which is the direct source of the time increase.

MiniCPMVProcessingInfo.get_hf_processor (and MiniCPMOProcessingInfo.get_hf_processor) now construct a fresh vendored `MiniCPMVProcessor`/`MiniCPMOProcessor` instance on every call. The underlying `self.ctx.get_hf_processor(...)` is cached via `cached_processor_from_config`/`lru_cache`, but the newly-added wrapping (`MiniCPMVProcessor(image_processor=..., tokenizer=...)` and the numpy->list conversion loop over mean/std) runs `ProcessorMixin.__init__` and reconstruction each time. `get_hf_processor` is invoked indirectly through `get_image_processor()` which the codebase calls repeatedly (get_slice_image_placeholder, get_image_size, dummy input builders, and per prompt-replacement in the multimodal processor). This turns a previously-cached lookup into per-call object construction. It is not on the token decode hot path, but it is per-request/per-item multimodal preprocessing work, so it can add measurable CPU overhead for image/audio-heavy workloads.

findings.claim: In the new generic ND x ND branch of matmul_batch_invariant, both operands are unconditionally passed through `.expand(...)` followed by `.reshape(batch_dim, -1, -1)`. When `broadcast_shape` actually broadcasts a size-1 batch dimension (e.g. the tested 3D x 4D / 2D x 4D / 4D x 3D / 2D x 5D patterns where one operand has a batch dim of 1 broadcast up to N), the expanded tensor is non-contiguous with stride-0 batch dims, so the subsequent `.reshape(...)` cannot return a view and silently materializes a full contiguous copy of the broadcast-expanded tensor. The old code only handled equal-rank / equal-batch cases (3D x 3D, 4D x 4D) via a plain `reshape` that stayed a view (contiguous), and the 2D x 3D case expanded `a` only into the small batch that bmm iterates. The new path can allocate/copy an O(broadcast_batch * M * K) buffer per call. If matmul_batch_invariant is on a hot inference path (e.g. Gemma-style 4D x 3D attention / linear layers), this is a per-call redundant buffer copy proportional to the broadcast factor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark matmul_batch_invariant on broadcasting shapes like a=(1,H,S,K), b=(H,K,N) and a=(S,K), b=(1,H,K,N) vs the old 4D x 4D path; measure allocated bytes / latency to confirm the expand+reshape copy overhead.

The new `os.fsync(fd)` added to `FastFileWriter._fini()` (deepspeed/io/fast_file_writer.py ~line 118) introduces a synchronous, blocking durability barrier on the checkpoint-save close path. `_fini()` runs once per save (via both `close()` and `__del__`), so in the target checkpoint-rotation loop this fsync executes every iteration, forcing a full flush of the just-written file (tens of GB/iter) to stable storage before close returns. The PR description itself quantifies this as ~5% wall-time overhead at ~10 GB/s peak. The bare `os.close(fd)` is what actually fixes the fd leak; the `os.fsync()` is an optional durability add (author notes removing only the fsync line still fixes the leak), so this is a deliberate correctness-vs-throughput tradeoff that regresses save throughput. Recommend making the fsync conditional (only on the unaligned-tail buffered re-open path, which is the only path needing it) rather than unconditionally on every close.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time N iterations of FastFileWriter save+close (aligned payload, ~GB scale) with vs without the os.fsync line to confirm the ~5% teardown-path overhead; also measure the aligned-only path where fsync provides no correctness benefit.

findings.claim: gemma4_mm.py `_clear_mm_prefix_for_full_attn_layers` is called on every `forward()` invocation (per decode/prefill step). For each call it does a `from vllm.forward_context import get_forward_context` import (cheap after first, but still a dict lookup), then iterates over ALL per-layer attn_metadata entries, string-splitting each `layer_name` (`.split('.layers.')`, `.split('.')`, `int(...)`) to recover the layer index. This is O(num_layers) Python string parsing every step. The precompute of `_full_attn_layer_idxs` in __init__ addresses the regex-per-call concern, and the function early-returns when the frozenset is empty (non-bidi models), so the hot-path cost is confined to Gemma4-bidi models. Still, per-layer-name string parsing every forward could be precomputed into a name->idx map once.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Gemma4 bidi decode latency (per-step) with many layers to measure per-call metadata-scan overhead

findings.claim: The new `build_inference_pg_collections_for_shards` (megatron/core/inference/shards.py) loops over every shard spec and calls `build_inference_pg_collection` for EACH shard on EVERY rank, even ranks not belonging to that shard. Each shard build issues ~13 `HyperCommGrid.create_pg` calls (each of which invokes the world-collective `dist.new_subgroups_by_enumeration`) plus a per-PP-group loop of `dist.new_group` calls (2 per PP group). Because `new_subgroups_by_enumeration`/`new_group` are world-collective, the total number of communicator-creation collectives grows linearly with the number of shards and every rank must participate in all of them — an O(num_shards) burst of NCCL communicator creations at init. This matches the 'redundant-collective' prior: work/collectives that every rank runs but only a subset needs. This is a one-time startup/init path (not a per-iteration hot path), so the impact is bounded to init latency / communicator memory, hence a suggestion rather than critical.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Init-time benchmark: measure wall-clock and NCCL communicator count for build_inference_pg_collections_for_shards with a growing number of shards (e.g. 1,2,4,8,16) at fixed world size; watch communicator-creation time and per-rank comm memory.

In `SpecDecodeBaseProposer._create_draft_vllm_config` (llm_base_proposer.py) the draft `attention_config.backend` is now ALWAYS overridden with `spec_cfg.attention_backend` (default `None`), whereas before the diff the draft config inherited the base `vllm_config.attention_config` unchanged. If the user explicitly selected a fast/specialized attention backend on the target (e.g. via `--attention-backend`), that backend is now discarded for the drafter and replaced by fresh auto-selection. For non-DFlash draft models (EAGLE/draft-model paths that were previously happy inheriting the target backend), this can silently swap the drafter to a slower auto-selected backend than the operator intended. The 'never inherit' comment is aimed at DFlash's non-causal requirement, but the code applies it unconditionally to all proposers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Spec-decode E2E draft-model latency with target using an explicitly chosen backend (e.g. FLASHINFER/FLASH_ATTN) — compare draft-step latency before/after to confirm the drafter still lands on the same backend.

mm_serde.py forces ALL multimodal tensor data inline via MsgpackEncoder(size_threshold=2**62) and then base64-encodes it into a JSON string on the render side (OpenAIServingRender._extract_mm_features -> encode_mm_kwargs_item, called per multimodal item, per request), and base64-decodes+msgpack-decodes it again on the generate side (ServingTokens -> decode_mm_kwargs_item). This is a per-request hot path in the render->generate disaggregated flow. Base64 inflates the (already large) pixel_values/embeds tensors by ~33% and forces a full inline copy through JSON, defeating the msgpack zero-copy/OOB-buffer optimization that MsgpackEncoder was designed for. For image/video features this can be MBs per request routed through Python string+JSON serialization on every generate call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: render->generate roundtrip latency with Qwen3-VL image inputs; measure serialize/deserialize time and payload size vs number/size of images

`_has_module` was changed from a lightweight `importlib.util.find_spec` availability probe to also performing a full `importlib.import_module(module_name)`. This means every optional-dependency detector built on it (has_deep_ep, has_deep_gemm, has_nixl_ep, has_triton_kernels, has_tilelang, has_arctic_inference, has_helion, has_aiter, has_mori, has_fbgemm_gpu, has_cutedsl) now eagerly imports and executes the top-level init of potentially heavy packages (loading native shared libraries, JIT/CUDA-adjacent setup) merely to answer an availability boolean. Previously these probes never ran module init code. This adds real one-time startup/first-call cost and partially defeats the lazy-loading intent behind these `has_*` gates. It is cache-bounded (@cache) so it is amortized once per module name rather than a hot-loop cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure vLLM engine cold-start / first-call time with optional deps (deep_gemm, aiter, fbgemm_gpu, triton_kernels) installed, comparing parent vs this commit; specifically time the first invocation of has_deep_gemm()/has_aiter() etc.

If this detector is consulted during backend/quant-method selection at startup, it can materially increase initialization latency on systems where deep_gemm is present but not otherwise needed for the chosen path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time has_deep_gemm() first call with deep_gemm installed, parent vs commit.

findings.claim: Both _process_complete_block_streaming and _process_partial_block_streaming call _build_tool_maps(request.tools) on EVERY streaming step. _build_tool_maps iterates all request.tools and rebuilds four dict/set structures (name_to_tool, name_to_allowed_props with set(props.keys()), name_to_required, tool_names) from the static per-request tool schema. These maps do not change during a request, yet they are reconstructed per token whenever a <function block is being streamed. This is init-shaped work (schema flattening) placed on the per-token hot path; it should be computed once per request (e.g. cached on the parser instance keyed by request identity or in adjust_request) rather than every delta.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Streaming tool-call request with a large tools list (e.g. 30+ tools, nested JSON-schema properties); compare per-token overhead with and without caching _build_tool_maps.

findings.claim: In the per-token streaming path extract_tool_calls_streaming (called once per generated token), the code calls current_text = _normalize_model_output(current_text) unconditionally. _normalize_model_output does multiple substring scans (`_TOKENIZER_SPACE not in text`, `<functionname=` not in text, etc.) and up to 4 `str.replace` passes over the ENTIRE accumulated current_text every token. Since current_text grows with each token, this is O(n) work per token → O(n^2) over the full response, redundantly re-normalizing the same prefix repeatedly. Peer parsers (e.g. internlm2_tool_parser) short-circuit early when the start token is absent and only inspect delta_text. Consider normalizing incrementally on delta_text or gating the replace passes behind a cheap membership check that is already partially present but still rescans the whole string.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Streaming chat completion with a long (>2k token) tool-call response using tool_parser=minicpm5; measure per-token latency / total decode time vs internlm2 parser.

findings.claim: In the streaming arg converter for DeepSeekV4/V32 (`_convert_args`), each call re-runs the full `_dsml_arg_converter` (which does `_PARAM_RE.finditer` over the *entire* accumulated `raw_args`) and then, only to find the function name, executes `next((s.name for s in self._tool_slots if s.args == raw_args), None)`. The generator materializes `s.args` for every tool slot (which itself joins `_args_parts` on first access) and compares full argument strings for equality on every streaming tick. The arg converter is invoked on the hot streaming path (parser_engine.py:919/962/1017 -> `converter = self._arg_converter`) for every ARG_VALUE_CHUNK delta, so as arguments grow, per-tick cost grows O(total_args_len) both for the regex re-scan and the per-slot string comparison — a quadratic re-scan pattern across the stream. The parent DeepSeekV32ToolParser tracked the active tool name/index directly instead of scanning slots by string equality.

CohereCommand `extract_content_ids` decodes an ever-growing token buffer on every token (`s = self.model_tokenizer.decode(token_buf, skip_special_tokens=False)` called with `token_buf` growing until a non-REPLACEMENT_CHAR flush). Since the buffer is only cleared when `s` does not end in REPLACEMENT_CHAR, in the common case where each token decodes cleanly the buffer is 1 token, but where multi-byte tokens accumulate the decode cost grows with the pending buffer. More importantly, unlike reference parsers (step3/deepseek) which do an O(n) `index()`/membership check, this method invokes a full tokenizer.decode() per input token — a per-token Python + tokenizer round-trip along a path invoked per decode step in structured/xgrammar output. Compare step3's `extract_content_ids` which is a single slice.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Streaming decode throughput with cohere_command3 reasoning parser + xgrammar structured output enabled, long generations (>512 tokens), measuring per-token latency vs a slice-based parser.

A custom `@model_serializer(mode="wrap")` is added to `DeltaMessage` (vllm/entrypoints/openai/engine/protocol.py) which is serialized once per streaming chunk in the per-token hot path. In `chat_completion/serving.py` each SSE chunk is produced via `chunk.model_dump_json(exclude_unset=True)` (lines ~533, 569, 751), and each chunk contains a `DeltaMessage` delta. Wrapping the serializer forces pydantic to route the model's serialization through a Python callable on every chunk (rather than the fully-Rust/compiled fast path), then perform an extra `data.get("tool_calls", [])` lookup + `len()` + `data.pop()` for every token emitted. This adds a small but per-token/per-chunk Python overhead to the streaming latency-critical path, whereas the intended effect (omitting empty tool_calls) could be achieved with a field-level `Field(exclude/serialization)` or default-None + exclude_defaults without a wrap serializer callback.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Streaming chat completions throughput/latency benchmark: measure tokens/s and per-chunk model_dump_json cost with many small deltas (e.g. 1000-token stream), before vs after adding the model_serializer, to quantify per-chunk serialization overhead.

The new block in get_computed_blocks() (kv_cache_manager.py ~line 236) adds a per-request loop over KV cache groups that, for each group with cache hits, calls block_pool.emit_cached_block_events(). Inside that method (block_pool.py emit_cached_block_events) there is a per-block loop that (a) rebuilds the block-hash view via resolve_block_hashes, (b) calls maybe_convert_block_hash for every cached block, and (c) calls generate_block_hash_extra_keys for every cached block. This runs on the per-request scheduler hot path (get_computed_blocks is called for every scheduled/waiting request) and does O(num_cached_blocks) work rebuilding event payloads for blocks that are simply prefix-cache reuses. For long prefixes with many cached blocks this materializes large token_id slices (request.all_token_ids[0:num_cached_blocks*block_size]) and per-block extra-key computations on every prefill scheduling pass. This is the classic 'redundant hot-path work' pattern (per-block work newly added to the request scheduling path).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark scheduler throughput with kv_cache_report_mode='full' vs 'incremental' on a workload with high prefix-cache hit rates and long shared prefixes (e.g. many requests sharing a long system prompt), measuring get_computed_blocks latency / scheduling step time.

findings.claim: emit_cached_block_events builds token_ids via request.all_token_ids[start_token_idx:end_token_idx] with end_token_idx = num_cached_blocks * block_size, i.e. it slices (copies) the entire reused prefix on every emission. Unlike cache_full_blocks which only slices the newly-cached delta [num_cached_blocks*bs : num_full_blocks*bs], this event copies the FULL cached prefix each scheduling pass, so cost grows with total prefix length rather than the incremental delta. Guarded by enable_kv_cache_events + report_mode=='full', so only affects that config, but it is a redundant large copy on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-request event-build cost (token_ids slice size) as a function of prefix length under full report mode.

findings.claim: In `get_encode_kwargs`, the condition that disables tokenizer-level truncation was broadened from `if self.truncation_side == "left"` to `if self.truncation_side is not None and self.truncate_prompt_tokens is not None`. This means that whenever a user passes `truncation_side="right"` (which is now newly exposed on the OpenAI chat/completion endpoints) together with `truncate_prompt_tokens`, the tokenizer is asked to encode WITHOUT truncation (`truncation=False, max_length` omitted). The full (untruncated) text is tokenized, and slicing to N tokens only happens later in the Python-level `_token_truncation`. For the common `truncation_side="right"` case this is a behavioral regression relative to the prior code path (which used fast tokenizer-native truncation with `max_length`), forcing the tokenizer to materialize the entire token sequence for a long prompt before Python slices it back down. The extra work scales with the full prompt length rather than the requested N. This is a minor, opt-in cost (only when both params are set) but is a genuinely redundant hot-path expansion versus letting the tokenizer truncate on the right natively.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark: tokenize a very long text prompt (e.g. 100k chars) via the completions/chat endpoint with truncate_prompt_tokens=8, truncation_side='right' vs no truncation_side, comparing tokenizer.encode wall time (native max_length truncation vs full encode + Python slice).

findings.claim: This diff removes `fastsafetensors @ git+...` from `requirements/rocm.txt` (the runtime deps for ROCm), so ROCm wheels/Docker images will no longer ship fastsafetensors by default. fastsafetensors is the GPU-accelerated safetensors weight loader (`--load-format fastsafetensors`) — it is imported lazily behind a try/except with PlaceholderModule (vllm/model_executor/model_loader/weight_utils.py:59-63), so removing the dependency does not break the build but silently makes the fast weight-load path unavailable on ROCm at runtime unless the user manually installs it. This is the exact inverse of the historical change that ENABLED fastsafetensors on ROCm by adding it here to reduce model weight-load/startup time; reverting it regresses ROCm cold-start/model-load time back to the slower default safetensors loader. The change is justified for compatibility (uv can't resolve git+ URLs), but there is a startup-time performance regression tradeoff: fastsafetensors is no longer available out-of-the-box on ROCm runtime images.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model weight load / TTFT (time-to-first-token) startup latency on a ROCm build with a large model comparing default safetensors loader vs `--load-format fastsafetensors`, before and after this change (with fastsafetensors absent from the runtime image).
